era_context: Late Old Kingdom Egypt (Dynasty VI), Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin, and mature Harappan civilization. This year falls within a period of transition: Egypt's centralized authority is fragmenting, the Akkadian Empire is at its maximum extent but facing internal and external pressures, and the Harappan civilization remains stable. These are the final decades before major political upheavals reshape the Near East and Egypt.

events.title: Pepi II of Egypt — late reign and Old Kingdom decline
events.description: Pepi II's reign continues in its final decades (if the traditional 64-year length is accepted; modern scholars debate this). The Old Kingdom's centralized authority has substantially eroded. Provincial governors operate with increasing independence, and the pharaoh's power is concentrated in the Memphis region. The administrative system that characterized the Old Kingdom is breaking down, and Egypt is moving toward the political fragmentation of the First Intermediate Period. This represents the end of an era of centralized state power that had lasted for over 300 years.
events.certainty_note: The decline of Old Kingdom authority is well-attested in contemporary sources. However, the exact dating of Pepi II's reign and its length are disputed by modern scholars. The year -2275 is assigned by convention.
cross_references: Precedes -2184-001 (end of Old Kingdom and beginning of First Intermediate Period)

events.title: Naram-Sin of Akkad — empire at maximum extent, internal pressures mounting
events.description: Naram-Sin's Akkadian Empire is at its maximum territorial extent, controlling vast territories from the Persian Gulf to Anatolia. However, the empire is facing mounting internal and external pressures. Rebellions in distant provinces require constant military suppression, and the Gutian threat from the northeast is increasing. The empire's administrative system is stretched to its limits, and the costs of maintaining such a vast territory are becoming unsustainable. Within a few decades, the empire will begin to collapse.
events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign and the empire's extent are well-documented in contemporary sources. The absolute dating is uncertain by ±10–20 years depending on the chronological system used.

events.title: Harappan civilization — mature phase continues with no visible decline
events.description: The Harappan civilization continues its stable mature phase with no archaeological evidence of decline or major changes in this year. Urban centers remain populated and organized, trade networks are active, and the civilization's characteristic material culture persists. The civilization will maintain this stable pattern for several more centuries before beginning to decline around 1900 BCE. The contrast between the stability of Harappan civilization and the political upheavals occurring in Egypt and Mesopotamia is striking.

sources.name: Indus script inscriptions on seals and pottery

events.certainty_note: The Harappan civilization's existence and characteristics are confirmed by extensive archaeological evidence. Year-specific events are not identifiable from the archaeological record.
cross_references: Part of broader mature Harappan phase (c. 2600–1900 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional length of Pepi II's reign (64 years) is increasingly questioned by modern Egyptologists, who suggest it may have been significantly shorter. This affects the absolute dating of events within his reign. The Akkadian chronology remains contested, with competing systems placing Naram-Sin's reign at different absolute dates (difference of ~60 years between long and short chronologies). The Indus script remains undeciphered, so claims about Harappan political organization or specific events are speculative.
historiographic_note: The historical record for -2275 is sparse and heavily dependent on Egyptian administrative inscriptions and Akkadian royal inscriptions, both of which are biased toward elite activities and propagandistic narratives. The Indus Valley is known almost entirely through archaeology, which cannot identify year-specific events or provide political narratives. The dating of all events depends on competing chronological systems, introducing uncertainty of ±10–50 years. The historical record is heavily skewed toward Mesopotamia and Egypt; other regions are virtually invisible in written sources. This year represents a transitional moment in Near Eastern history, but the exact chronology and causation of events remain uncertain.

graph_edges.note: Pepi II's reign and the decentralization of Old Kingdom authority led to the collapse of the Old Kingdom and the First Intermediate Period
Naram-Sin's reign ends around 2218 BCE, followed by the rapid decline of the Akkadian Empire
The pressures mounting on the Akkadian Empire during Naram-Sin's reign lead to its collapse around 2154 BCE
The stable Harappan civilization eventually begins to decline around 1900 BCE
Pepi II of Egypt and Naram-Sin of Akkad are contemporaries, ruling during the same period
